transitiontrees::bootstrap_pathways() — demonstration

2026-06-02

What bootstrap_pathways() reports

Methodologically built on Saqr, Tikka & López-Pernas (2025) tna, extending the edge-level bootstrap framework to variable-depth pathways.

The bootstrap operates on raw counts only — no nmin filter, no smoothing inside the loop, no extra parameters. Each iteration resamples whole sequences, aggregates counts per depth, and reads each original-tree pathway’s count vector directly.

Two complementary measures per pathway:

  • stability_rate — fraction of resamples where the chosen stat (default count) lands in [0.75 × observed, 1.25 × observed]. Value reproducibility. A pathway whose count drops to zero in a resample fails the band test automatically — same mechanism tna::bootstrap uses for edge weights.

  • informative_rate — fraction of resamples where the pathway’s empirical likelihood-ratio statistic against its parent context exceeds the chi-square critical value at alpha = 0.05. Reproducible significant divergence from the shorter-history baseline. The bootstrap analogue of prune(criterion = "G2").

A pathway is stable when stability_rate ≥ stability_threshold (default 0.75) and informative when informative_rate ≥ informative_threshold (default 0.95). The summary sorts stable + informative pathways first.

Validation: strong, weak, random

Three Markov regimes from Saqrlab::simulate_sequences(), same sample size (100 sequences × 14 length, alphabet {A, B, C}):

P_strong <- rbind(c(0.05, 0.90, 0.05),
                  c(0.05, 0.05, 0.90),
                  c(0.90, 0.05, 0.05))
P_weak   <- rbind(c(0.30, 0.50, 0.20),
                  c(0.20, 0.30, 0.50),
                  c(0.50, 0.20, 0.30))
P_random <- matrix(1 / 3, 3L, 3L)
rownames(P_strong) <- colnames(P_strong) <- c("A", "B", "C")
rownames(P_weak)   <- colnames(P_weak)   <- c("A", "B", "C")
rownames(P_random) <- colnames(P_random) <- c("A", "B", "C")
init <- c(A = 1/3, B = 1/3, C = 1/3)

run_scenario <- function(P, seed) {
  seqs <- simulate_sequences(
    n_sequences = 100L, seq_length = 14L,
    trans_matrix = P, init_probs = init,
    use_learning_states = FALSE, include_na = FALSE,
    seed = seed)
  tree <- context_tree(seqs, max_depth = 2L, min_count = 5L)
  bootstrap_pathways(tree, iter = 300L, seed = 1L)
}

b_strong <- run_scenario(P_strong, seed = 1L)
b_weak   <- run_scenario(P_weak,   seed = 2L)
b_random <- run_scenario(P_random, seed = 3L)

Print: STRONG (modal probability 0.90)

b_strong
## <transitiontrees_bootstrap>  300 resamples
##   stability  : count in [0.75, 1.25] x observed, p < 0.25
##   informative: G^2 > qchisq(0.95, df=k-1) = 5.99, threshold 0.95
##   pathways   : 13 total, 10 stable, 3 informative, 3 both
## 
## top pathways (stable + informative first):
##  pathway depth count p_stability stability_rate stable informative_rate
##        C     1   438       0.003          1.000   TRUE            1.000
##        A     1   436       0.003          1.000   TRUE            1.000
##        B     1   426       0.003          1.000   TRUE            1.000
##   (root)     0  1400       0.003          1.000   TRUE               NA
##   C -> A     2   368       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.000
##   B -> C     2   352       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.000
##   A -> B     2   348       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.003
##   B -> B     2    25       0.136          0.867   TRUE            0.257
##   C -> C     2    20       0.193          0.810   TRUE            0.063
##   C -> B     2    25       0.233          0.770   TRUE            0.120
##  informative mean_G2 ci_G2_lo ci_G2_hi
##         TRUE 610.320  550.600  677.821
##         TRUE 590.714  545.239  637.437
##         TRUE 583.347  536.553  633.219
##        FALSE     NaN       NA       NA
##        FALSE   0.543    0.014    1.808
##        FALSE   0.641    0.046    1.970
##        FALSE   0.769    0.032    2.572
##        FALSE   5.026    2.016   10.833
##        FALSE   3.332    1.241    7.091
##        FALSE   3.187    0.096    9.167
## # ... 3 more pathways (use summary(x) for full table)

Three depth-1 pathways (A, B, C) have G² ~600 with tight CIs — their conditional distribution differs dramatically from the marginal, so informative_rate = 1. Depth-2 pathways have G² near zero because the data is 1st-order Markov: conditional on the most recent state, prior history adds nothing. The bootstrap correctly flags depth-1 as informative and depth-2 as not — the latter would be the wrong choice in a longer-memory tree.

Print: WEAK (modal probability 0.50)

b_weak
## <transitiontrees_bootstrap>  300 resamples
##   stability  : count in [0.75, 1.25] x observed, p < 0.25
##   informative: G^2 > qchisq(0.95, df=k-1) = 5.99, threshold 0.95
##   pathways   : 13 total, 13 stable, 3 informative, 3 both
## 
## top pathways (stable + informative first):
##  pathway depth count p_stability stability_rate stable informative_rate
##        B     1   455       0.003          1.000   TRUE            1.000
##        A     1   434       0.003          1.000   TRUE            1.000
##        C     1   411       0.003          1.000   TRUE            1.000
##   (root)     0  1400       0.003          1.000   TRUE               NA
##   A -> B     2   211       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.000
##   B -> C     2   208       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.023
##   C -> A     2   182       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.033
##   B -> A     2   103       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.033
##   C -> B     2    89       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.033
##   B -> B     2   116       0.007          0.997   TRUE            0.097
##  informative mean_G2 ci_G2_lo ci_G2_hi
##         TRUE  59.077   35.472   82.717
##         TRUE  61.262   38.464   86.434
##         TRUE  51.363   28.872   77.407
##        FALSE     NaN       NA       NA
##        FALSE   1.134    0.030    3.884
##        FALSE   1.978    0.100    5.802
##        FALSE   1.867    0.041    6.364
##        FALSE   1.709    0.066    6.440
##        FALSE   1.688    0.050    6.369
##        FALSE   2.629    0.090    8.778
## # ... 3 more pathways (use summary(x) for full table)

Same structure, weaker signal. Depth-1 G² ~50–60: still well above the chi-square critical value (5.99), so depth-1 is reproducibly informative. Depth-2 G² ~1–4 sits below the critical value most of the time, so depth-2 is reproducibly not informative — exactly right for 1st-order Markov.

Print: RANDOM (uniform 1/3)

b_random
## <transitiontrees_bootstrap>  300 resamples
##   stability  : count in [0.75, 1.25] x observed, p < 0.25
##   informative: G^2 > qchisq(0.95, df=k-1) = 5.99, threshold 0.95
##   pathways   : 13 total, 13 stable, 0 informative, 0 both
## 
## top pathways (stable + informative first):
##  pathway depth count p_stability stability_rate stable informative_rate
##   (root)     0  1400       0.003          1.000   TRUE               NA
##        C     1   446       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.860
##        B     1   429       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.453
##        A     1   425       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.923
##   A -> A     2   159       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.047
##   C -> B     2   153       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.010
##   A -> C     2   131       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.003
##   B -> A     2   127       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.123
##   B -> C     2   117       0.003          1.000   TRUE            0.030
##   B -> B     2   142       0.010          0.993   TRUE            0.047
##  informative mean_G2 ci_G2_lo ci_G2_hi
##        FALSE     NaN       NA       NA
##        FALSE  12.432    3.130   25.564
##        FALSE   5.913    0.703   15.208
##        FALSE  13.959    4.512   27.035
##        FALSE   1.951    0.061    6.614
##        FALSE   1.453    0.036    4.876
##        FALSE   1.673    0.042    5.167
##        FALSE   2.973    0.138    8.837
##        FALSE   2.060    0.055    6.486
##        FALSE   1.812    0.043    7.192
## # ... 3 more pathways (use summary(x) for full table)

All counts are reproducible (stable = TRUE everywhere — counts of uniform random transitions reproduce just fine), but zero pathways are informative at the strict 0.95 threshold. The chi-square test is hovering at finite-sample chance levels (G² ~5–14, near or just above the critical value), and the bootstrap correctly says it doesn’t reproducibly clear the threshold.

Headline numbers across regimes

cmp <- function(b, label) {
  s <- b$summary
  data.frame(
    scenario      = label,
    n_pathways    = nrow(s),
    n_stable      = sum(s$stable, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_informative = sum(s$informative, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_both        = sum(s$stable & s$informative, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_G2     = round(median(s$mean_G2, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    mean_G2       = round(mean(s$mean_G2, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p95_G2        = round(quantile(s$mean_G2, 0.95,
                                    na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  )
}
rbind(
  cmp(b_strong, "STRONG (modal=0.90)"),
  cmp(b_weak,   "WEAK   (modal=0.50)"),
  cmp(b_random, "RANDOM (uniform)")
)

mean_G2 orders cleanly across regimes (151 / 16 / 4) and n_informative orders the same way (3 / 3 / 0) at the strict threshold. n_stable is high in all three because counts are reproducible regardless of underlying structure — that’s stability’s job; informativeness is the test that distinguishes real signal.

Forest plots

plot(b_strong)

plot(b_weak)

plot(b_random, min_stability = 0)

The dashed line marks the chi-square critical value. STRONG’s depth-1 pathways sit far to the right of it; WEAK’s still clear it comfortably; RANDOM’s straddle or fall below it.

Resample distributions of G²

plot_pathway_resamples(b_strong, stat = "G2", top = 6L)

plot_pathway_resamples(b_random, stat = "G2", top = 6L)

STRONG’s depth-1 G² distributions are tight at 500–700; depth-2 distributions sit near zero. RANDOM shows everything piled up near the chi-square critical value with wide spread — exactly what finite-sample noise produces.

Real data: Nestimate::trajectories

data("trajectories", package = "Nestimate")
dim(trajectories)
## [1] 138  15
tree <- context_tree(trajectories, max_depth = 3L, min_count = 5L)
boot <- bootstrap_pathways(tree, iter = 500L, seed = 1L)
boot
## <transitiontrees_bootstrap>  500 resamples
##   stability  : count in [0.75, 1.25] x observed, p < 0.25
##   informative: G^2 > qchisq(0.95, df=k-1) = 5.99, threshold 0.95
##   pathways   : 38 total, 26 stable, 9 informative, 9 both
## 
## top pathways (stable + informative first):
##                      pathway depth count p_stability stability_rate stable
##                      Average     1   751       0.002          1.000   TRUE
##            Active -> Average     2   160       0.002          1.000   TRUE
##                       Active     1   658       0.004          0.998   TRUE
##            Average -> Active     2   144       0.006          0.996   TRUE
##                   Disengaged     1   325       0.020          0.982   TRUE
##             Active -> Active     2   433       0.028          0.974   TRUE
##        Disengaged -> Average     2   122       0.028          0.974   TRUE
##  Average -> Active -> Active     3    70       0.040          0.962   TRUE
##     Disengaged -> Disengaged     2   139       0.174          0.828   TRUE
##                       (root)     0  1870       0.002          1.000   TRUE
##  informative_rate informative mean_G2 ci_G2_lo ci_G2_hi
##             1.000        TRUE 119.906   75.898  171.678
##             0.996        TRUE  28.781   12.718   48.649
##             1.000        TRUE 320.335  253.141  395.531
##             1.000        TRUE  30.522   12.497   52.917
##             1.000        TRUE 181.365  111.871  262.649
##             0.992        TRUE  18.063    7.715   29.959
##             1.000        TRUE  32.303   13.445   53.598
##             0.994        TRUE  24.213    9.799   43.740
##             0.996        TRUE  21.624    9.183   37.531
##                NA       FALSE     NaN       NA       NA
## # ... 28 more pathways (use summary(x) for full table)
plot(boot)

plot_pathway_resamples(boot, stat = "G2", top = 6L)

summary(boot)

Design notes

  • Bootstrap operates on raw counts. No nmin filter, no smoothing inside the loop, no extra parameters. Pathways tracked are exactly the ones in the original tree; their values come from per-depth count aggregation.
  • stable answers “does the value reproduce?” — tna’s framing. With enough data, counts reproduce regardless of whether the underlying structure is informative.
  • informative answers “does the LR test reproducibly clear the threshold?” — the bootstrap analogue of prune(criterion = "G2"). This is the test that distinguishes real signal from finite-sample noise.
  • Use both flags together. A pathway worth interpreting is one where stable AND informative. The summary sorts those first.
  • No prune() inside the resample loop. Pruning is a downstream filter on the bootstrap output, not part of the fit replay.
  • Performance. Per-sequence (context, next_state) pair-index vectors are precomputed once per depth (Nestimate’s transition fast-path). Bootstrap iterations are one unlist+tabulate per depth followed by per-pathway count lookups, with no data.frame construction inside the loop.
